packTitle: Natural Disasters and Medical Safety Import Batch
notes: This batch normalizes Perplexity research into Replit-ready guide objects.
Guide types have been normalized to action_card, scenario_guide, preparedness_guide, or reference_guide.
Integration decisions are best-effort recommendations against the current 57-guide app library.
Landslide guidance is included but marked as mixed source quality and needs_source_review.

categories.summary: Normalized natural-disaster guides compiled from Perplexity research with strong public-safety sources for earthquakes, floods, tornadoes, wildfire smoke/evacuation, and landslides.

guides.title: During Earthquake Shaking
guides.quickAnswer: Drop, Cover, and Hold On under sturdy furniture or next to an interior wall away from glass and falling objects.
guides.whenToUse: Use when you feel shaking, receive an earthquake alert, or experience a sudden jolt that makes standing difficult.
preferredAction: Drop to your hands and knees.
Cover your head and neck under a sturdy table or desk if available.
Hold on until the shaking stops.
backupAction: If no sturdy furniture is available, cover your head and neck and get low beside an interior wall away from windows and heavy objects.
steps: Drop where you are.
Get under sturdy furniture if available.
Cover your head and neck.
Hold on until all shaking stops.
Stay inside until the shaking ends unless you are already in a clear outdoor area.
warnings: Expect aftershocks.
Falling glass, shelves, and fixtures cause many injuries.
whatNotToDo: Do not run outside while the building is shaking.
Do not use elevators.
Do not stand in doorways as your main strategy.
redFlags: Serious injuries, heavy bleeding, major structural damage, or gas smell require urgent emergency help.
If near the coast and shaking was strong or long, be alert for tsunami evacuation needs as soon as you can move safely.
preparednessTips: Practice earthquake drills.
Secure heavy furniture and appliances.
Know how to turn off utilities if trained and safe to do so.

sourceReferences.title: CDC Earthquake Safety Guidance
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Clear public guidance for what to do during shaking in different environments.

sourceReferences.title: Earthquake Safety
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Plain-language before, during, and after steps for households.

sourceReferences.title: Earthquake Preparedness and Response
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Practical hazard and preparedness guidance relevant to homes and workplaces.

guides.title: After Earthquake
guides.quickAnswer: Once shaking stops, check for injuries and hazards before moving or re-entering any damaged area.
guides.whenToUse: Use when the shaking has stopped and you need to decide whether to stay, leave, or seek help.
preferredAction: Check yourself and others for injuries.
Look for hazards such as gas leaks, fire, sparks, broken glass, and structural damage.
Listen for official updates and aftershock warnings.
backupAction: If the building looks unsafe, stay out and move to a safer open area away from structures and power lines.
steps: Treat urgent injuries if you can do so safely.
If you smell gas or see fire, evacuate immediately.
Do not use candles if gas leaks are possible.
Use a flashlight for dark areas.
Be ready for aftershocks and repeat Drop, Cover, and Hold On if needed.
warnings: Aftershocks can cause additional collapses.
Damaged wiring, fuel leaks, and unstable structures remain dangerous after the main shaking.
whatNotToDo: Do not re-enter heavily damaged buildings.
Do not use open flames near possible gas leaks.
Do not ignore official evacuation or coastal tsunami guidance.
redFlags: Gas leaks, fire, collapse risk, downed power lines, or serious injuries need urgent help.
preparednessTips: Keep sturdy shoes, a flashlight, and emergency supplies accessible.
Have a family meeting point and an out-of-area contact.

sourceReferences.title: Earthquake Safety
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Strong before/during/after guidance for families.

sourceReferences.title: Earthquake Preparedness and Response
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Useful checklists for hazards, re-entry, and utilities.

guides.title: Earthquake Preparedness Checklist
guides.quickAnswer: Prepare before a quake by securing heavy items, practicing drills, and keeping essential supplies and utility knowledge ready.
guides.whenToUse: Use before earthquakes as part of household, workplace, or school readiness planning.
preferredAction: Secure heavy furniture and appliances.
Practice Drop, Cover, and Hold On.
Store an emergency kit and shoes/flashlight near sleeping areas.
backupAction: If you cannot do everything, start with securing the most dangerous heavy items and building a simple family plan.
steps: Anchor bookshelves and cabinets.
Move heavy items to lower shelves.
Know how to turn off gas, electricity, and water if safe and trained.
Build and refresh an emergency kit.
Practice where to shelter in each room.
warnings: Unsecured furniture and glass are major indoor injury hazards.
Aftershocks can injure people even after the first quake has passed.
whatNotToDo: Do not wait until after an earthquake to create your family plan.
Do not store all emergency supplies in one hard-to-reach place.
redFlags: If your home shows major structural weakness or known earthquake risk issues, seek professional safety advice.
preparednessTips: Keep local maps and emergency contacts accessible.
Include supplies for children, older adults, pets, and medications.

sourceReferences.title: CDC Earthquake Safety Guidance
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Concise planning advice for homes and families.

sourceReferences.title: Earthquake Safety
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Household drills, planning, and kit guidance.

guides.title: During Flood Warning
guides.quickAnswer: Move to higher ground immediately and follow evacuation orders. Never walk or drive through floodwater.
guides.whenToUse: Use during flood warnings, flash floods, fast-rising water, or when water is entering roads, buildings, or low areas.
preferredAction: Go to higher ground immediately.
Follow official evacuation orders without delay.
Turn off utilities if safe to do so before leaving.
backupAction: If you cannot evacuate safely, move to the highest safe level of the building, but avoid attics without a way out.
steps: Monitor alerts by radio or phone.
If safe, shut off utilities.
Take essentials and leave early.
Avoid flooded roads and low crossings.
Move to higher ground or official shelter.
warnings: Floodwater rises quickly and may hide strong currents, contamination, and electrical hazards.
Six inches of moving water can knock a person down; deeper water can move vehicles.
whatNotToDo: Do not walk, swim, or drive through floodwater.
Do not ignore barriers or evacuation orders.
redFlags: If trapped, water is entering the home, or someone is swept away or injured, call emergency services immediately.
preparednessTips: Know evacuation routes.
Store a 3-day kit with water, medications, and documents.

sourceReferences.title: Turn Around Don't Drown
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Core floodwater survival rule and practical safety framing.

sourceReferences.title: Flood Safety and Preparedness
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Strong before/during/after public guidance.

sourceReferences.title: Floodwater Safety
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Contamination and injury prevention guidance.

guides.title: After Flood
guides.quickAnswer: Avoid floodwater and damaged structures until hazards are checked. Treat all floodwater as contaminated.
guides.whenToUse: Use after floodwater recedes or when returning to a flooded home, neighborhood, or road.
preferredAction: Stay out until officials say it is safe where possible.
Avoid contact with floodwater.
Check for structural damage, electrical hazards, and contamination before re-entry.
backupAction: If return is delayed or the structure is unsafe, use temporary shelter and wait for official guidance.
steps: Wear protective gear if returning.
Do not touch downed power lines or wet electrical systems.
Clean wounds exposed to floodwater promptly.
Use safe drinking water only; boil or treat if advised.
Photograph damage if needed for records once safe.
warnings: Floodwater may contain sewage, chemicals, sharp debris, and electrical hazards.
Hidden structural damage may cause collapse or falls.
whatNotToDo: Do not enter buildings with major visible damage alone.
Do not touch wet electrical equipment.
Do not assume water or food is safe after flooding.
redFlags: Electrical shocks, serious injuries, infected wounds, gas leaks, or unstable buildings need urgent help.
preparednessTips: Keep waterproof storage for documents and supplies.
Store gloves, boots, flashlights, and cleaning supplies in your emergency kit.

sourceReferences.title: Floodwater Safety
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Health risks and cleanup safety.

sourceReferences.title: Flood Safety
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Plain-language before/during/after flood guidance.

guides.title: Driving in Flood Conditions
guides.quickAnswer: Never drive through flooded roads. Turn around, find another route, and move to higher ground if needed.
guides.whenToUse: Use when roads are flooded, rainfall is intense, or visibility and drainage conditions are poor.
preferredAction: Turn around and find a safer route.
Abandon a stalled vehicle in rising water if you can safely move to higher ground.
backupAction: If you cannot safely leave the vehicle and water is not yet rising into it, call for help and monitor conditions closely.
steps: Slow down and assess conditions before entering low areas.
Do not cross water-covered roads.
If your car stalls in rising water, get out if safe and move to higher ground.
Avoid bridges or roads that look damaged.
warnings: Moving water can sweep vehicles away in surprisingly shallow depths.
Road damage may be hidden beneath floodwater.
whatNotToDo: Do not drive around barricades.
Do not assume you can judge depth or road condition from the driver seat.
redFlags: If trapped in rising water, call emergency services immediately.
preparednessTips: Know alternate routes.
Keep a charged phone and emergency kit in the car during flood season.

sourceReferences.title: Turn Around Don't Drown
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Best single-source public rule for flood driving safety.

guides.title: Floodwater Safety
guides.quickAnswer: Treat all floodwater as contaminated and dangerous. Avoid direct contact whenever possible.
guides.whenToUse: Use when walking near, cleaning after, or being exposed to floodwater.
preferredAction: Avoid floodwater whenever possible.
Wash exposed skin and clean wounds promptly if contact happens.
backupAction: If exposure is unavoidable, use boots, gloves, and the safest route possible, then clean up immediately afterward.
steps: Stay out of floodwater when possible.
If contact occurs, wash with clean water and soap as soon as possible.
Cover open wounds before exposure if you can.
Discard or disinfect contaminated items according to public-health guidance.
warnings: Floodwater may contain sewage, chemicals, sharps, and hidden electrical risks.
whatNotToDo: Do not let children play in floodwater.
Do not assume clear-looking water is safe.
redFlags: Seek urgent help for infected wounds, electrical exposure, chemical burns, or severe illness after exposure.
preparednessTips: Keep protective gloves, boots, and wound-care supplies available during flood season.

sourceReferences.title: Floodwater Safety
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Primary health guidance on contamination and cleanup after floods.

guides.title: During Tornado Warning
guides.quickAnswer: Get to the lowest interior room of a sturdy building and protect your head and neck immediately.
guides.whenToUse: Use during a tornado warning, when a tornado is sighted, or when there are clear tornado danger signs.
preferredAction: Go to a basement, storm shelter, or lowest interior room.
Put as many walls as possible between you and the outside.
Protect your head and neck.
backupAction: If no basement is available, use a small interior room such as a closet, hallway, or bathroom on the lowest floor.
steps: Move quickly to the safest room.
Get low under sturdy furniture if available.
Cover your head and neck with your arms, a helmet, or a mattress/blanket if available.
Stay there until the all-clear.
warnings: Flying debris causes many tornado injuries.
Mobile homes are especially dangerous.
whatNotToDo: Do not shelter under overpasses.
Do not stay near windows.
Do not wait to visually confirm the tornado if an official warning is issued.
redFlags: Severe injuries, collapse, or trapped people require emergency help as soon as safe.
preparednessTips: Plan a safe room in advance.
Practice tornado drills twice yearly.
Keep a weather radio and helmets or head protection in your shelter area.

sourceReferences.title: Tornado Safety
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Best direct public guidance for shelter decisions.

sourceReferences.title: Tornadoes
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Strong before/during/after preparedness guidance.

sourceReferences.title: Stay Safe During a Tornado
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Scenario-based health and safety steps.

guides.title: After Tornado
guides.quickAnswer: After winds stop, watch for debris, injuries, gas leaks, and downed power lines before moving through damaged areas.
guides.whenToUse: Use after the tornado passes and you are deciding whether it is safe to leave shelter or help others.
preferredAction: Check injuries and immediate hazards first.
Watch for further tornado warnings or severe weather.
Avoid badly damaged areas unless rescue is essential and safe.
backupAction: If the structure is unsafe, move carefully to safer shelter and follow emergency instructions.
steps: Use shoes and gloves if available.
Avoid sharp debris and unstable buildings.
Report utility problems if possible.
Monitor radio or official alerts for updates.
warnings: Additional tornadoes may follow the first.
Debris, dust, gas leaks, and damaged wiring remain dangerous after the storm.
whatNotToDo: Do not enter heavily damaged buildings alone.
Do not touch downed lines or damaged electrical systems.
redFlags: Stopped breathing, severe bleeding, trapped people, gas smell, or collapse hazards require emergency help.
preparednessTips: Keep dust masks, flashlights, and sturdy footwear in your shelter kit.

sourceReferences.title: Stay Safe During a Tornado
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Post-event hazard and injury guidance.

sourceReferences.title: Tornadoes
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Preparedness and recovery guidance.

guides.title: Tornado Safety in a Vehicle
guides.quickAnswer: If possible, drive to a sturdy shelter immediately. Do not try to shelter under an overpass.
guides.whenToUse: Use when a tornado warning occurs while you are in a car or on the road.
preferredAction: Drive to the nearest sturdy building if there is time and a safe route.
If no sturdy shelter is reachable, follow official guidance for the safest available option and protect your head and neck.
backupAction: If escape is impossible and conditions demand immediate protection, move to the lowest safe place available away from vehicles and debris if you can do so safely.
steps: Look for the nearest sturdy building.
Do not stop under bridges or overpasses.
If you can reach shelter safely, do so immediately.
If not, protect your head and neck and follow local emergency guidance.
warnings: Vehicles are poor tornado shelters.
Road visibility and debris may change very quickly.
whatNotToDo: Do not try to outrun a tornado if traffic, storm movement, or visibility make it unsafe.
Do not shelter under overpasses.
redFlags: If the vehicle is damaged, overturned, or you are injured, call emergency services when possible.
preparednessTips: Plan frequent weather checks during tornado season.
Know sturdy shelter options along regular routes.

sourceReferences.title: Tornado Safety
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Scenario-specific vehicle guidance from the core authority.

sourceReferences.title: Tornadoes
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Preparedness and shelter guidance.

guides.title: Tornado Safety in a Mobile Home
guides.quickAnswer: Leave mobile homes early for a sturdy shelter when tornado warnings are issued. Mobile homes are not safe tornado shelters.
guides.whenToUse: Use when you are in or near a mobile home during a tornado watch or warning.
preferredAction: Go to a sturdy building or designated shelter immediately.
Have a preplanned shelter location before severe weather starts.
backupAction: If you cannot reach sturdy shelter in time, move to the safest available low area away from trees and vehicles if that is your only option.
steps: Monitor alerts closely.
Leave for shelter early rather than waiting to confirm the tornado visually.
Protect your head and neck once in shelter.
warnings: Mobile homes can overturn or fail even in weaker tornadoes and straight-line winds.
whatNotToDo: Do not stay inside a mobile home during a tornado warning.
Do not wait until debris is already visible if a warning is active.
redFlags: If someone is trapped, injured, or shelter cannot be reached safely, call emergency services as soon as possible.
preparednessTips: Know your nearest sturdy shelter and route in advance.
Keep a go-bag and weather radio ready.

sourceReferences.title: Tornado Safety
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Clear public guidance that mobile homes are unsafe shelters.

sourceReferences.title: Tornadoes
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Preparedness and shelter decision guidance.

guides.title: During Wildfire Smoke
guides.quickAnswer: Stay indoors, seal the home as much as possible, and use cleaner-air strategies. Use an N95 only if you must go outside briefly.
guides.whenToUse: Use during wildfire smoke events, air-quality alerts, or when smoke is present nearby even without flames in the area.
preferredAction: Stay inside and keep indoor air as clean as possible.
Use HEPA filtration or recirculating air if available.
Limit outdoor activity.
backupAction: If you must go outside briefly, use a well-fitting N95 if available and return to cleaner air as soon as possible.
steps: Close windows and doors.
Set AC to recirculate if available.
Use a HEPA filter or improvised cleaner-air room if possible.
Avoid indoor burning or activities that worsen indoor air.
warnings: Smoke can worsen asthma, COPD, heart disease, and other breathing problems.
Children, older adults, pregnant people, and those with chronic illness are higher risk.
whatNotToDo: Do not exercise outdoors in heavy smoke.
Do not rely on a wet cloth as meaningful smoke-particle protection.
Do not vacuum without suitable filtration during smoky conditions.
redFlags: Seek urgent medical help for chest pain, severe breathing trouble, blue lips, confusion, or worsening chronic symptoms.
preparednessTips: Stock N95s if appropriate for your household.
Keep medicines, inhalers, and a plan for cleaner indoor air.

sourceReferences.title: Wildfire Evacuation Outreach Materials
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Strong practical wildfire public guidance.

sourceReferences.title: How Wildfire Smoke Affects Your Body
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Health-risk guidance and higher-risk population advice.

sourceReferences.title: Public Health Advice During Wildfires
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Clear smoke symptom and protection guidance.

guides.title: Wildfire Evacuation
guides.quickAnswer: Leave early when told to evacuate or when conditions are becoming unsafe. Do not wait until roads are blocked or smoke is overwhelming.
guides.whenToUse: Use when evacuation orders are issued, fire is approaching, or authorities say conditions are unsafe.
preferredAction: Evacuate promptly using planned routes.
Take your go-bag, medications, pets, and essential documents.
backupAction: If evacuation is temporarily impossible, move to the safest available cleaner-air or official shelter location and monitor alerts continuously.
steps: Follow local alerts.
Load go-bags, medications, and pet supplies.
Back the car in if you have time and keep the tank sufficiently fueled.
Drive with windows up and ventilation recirculating if smoky.
warnings: Fires can spread rapidly and block roads.
Smoke can reduce visibility and impair breathing even ahead of flames.
whatNotToDo: Do not wait until the last minute.
Do not return until officials say it is safe.
redFlags: If fire is approaching, roads are cut off, or someone has severe breathing trouble or burns, seek emergency help immediately.
preparednessTips: Maintain mapped evacuation routes.
Prepare pet plans and a ready go-bag before wildfire season.

sourceReferences.title: Wildfire Evacuation Outreach Materials
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Best practical wildfire evacuation public guidance.

sourceReferences.title: How to Prepare for Emergencies
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Strong evacuation and readiness planning guidance.

guides.title: During Landslide
guides.quickAnswer: Move quickly out of the slide path toward safer stable ground. Watch for additional slides or flooding.
guides.whenToUse: Use when you hear, see, or suspect an active landslide, slope failure, or debris flow.
preferredAction: Move away from the slide path immediately.
Aim for more stable ground and avoid valleys or channels if debris is moving there.
backupAction: If escape is not possible, protect yourself and move to the safest available position away from the moving debris.
steps: Move out of the path as quickly as possible.
Warn others if you can do so safely.
Avoid river channels or low paths where debris may continue moving.
Once safe, monitor official updates.
warnings: Landslides may trigger floods, secondary slides, and structural collapse.
whatNotToDo: Do not remain in the slide path.
Do not re-enter unstable ground too quickly.
redFlags: Ground cracking, rumbling, sudden slope movement, trapped people, or damaged structures require urgent emergency help.
preparednessTips: Monitor saturated slopes during intense rain.
Know local high-risk areas and evacuation routes.

sourceReferences.title: Landslide Safety Guidance
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Practical public-safety style guidance, but source set should be strengthened before top-tier ranking.

guides.title: After Landslide
guides.quickAnswer: Stay away from unstable ground and damaged structures until authorities or qualified experts say it is safe.
guides.whenToUse: Use after the slide has stopped and you are deciding whether to return, search, or inspect damage.
preferredAction: Keep clear of the affected area.
Watch for more slides, flooding, and damaged utilities.
backupAction: If re-entry is delayed, use safer shelter and wait for official or expert advice.
steps: Check for trapped or injured people only if it is safe.
Report utility damage if possible.
Listen for emergency updates.
Do not enter visibly unstable areas.
warnings: Additional slides may follow the first event.
Unstable buildings, road edges, and buried utilities remain hazardous.
whatNotToDo: Do not enter unstable structures.
Do not assume the slope is stable because movement has stopped once.
redFlags: Structural cracks, trapped people, gas leaks, downed lines, or flooding require urgent emergency help.
preparednessTips: Consult geotechnical or local hazard resources if you live in a landslide-prone area.

sourceReferences.title: Post-Landslide Safety Guidance
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Reasonable public-safety principles, but source base should be reviewed and strengthened.

categories.summary: Normalized medical-safety guides compiled from Perplexity research with strong first-aid and public-health sources; conservative, non-invasive public guidance.

guides.title: Controlling Severe Bleeding (External)
guides.existingGuideReference: Severe Bleeding: Apply Direct Pressure Fast
guides.quickAnswer: Press hard directly on the wound, keep constant pressure, and get emergency help for life-threatening bleeding.
guides.whenToUse: Use for large or deep wounds with heavy, fast, or pooling bleeding, soaked dressings, or amputations.
preferredAction: Call emergency services early while maintaining pressure.
Press directly on the wound with a clean cloth or dressing.
Use a tourniquet only if severe limb bleeding continues and you are trained and it is available.
backupAction: If no bandages are available, use any clean cloth, clothing, or your hands directly over the wound.
steps: Ensure the scene is safe.
Put on gloves if available.
Expose the wound if safe.
Press firmly with both hands over the wound.
If blood soaks through, add more dressings on top.
Keep the person lying flat and warm.
Use a commercial tourniquet above the wound on a limb only if trained and needed for life-threatening bleeding.
warnings: Severe bleeding can be rapidly fatal.
Once a tourniquet is applied, it should not be loosened in the field unless directed by professionals.
whatNotToDo: Do not keep checking under the dressing.
Do not remove soaked dressings.
Do not delay calling emergency services to see if it stops.
redFlags: Spurting or pooling blood, shock signs, amputation, or major crush injuries need urgent help.
preparednessTips: Stock gloves, pressure dressings, and a commercial tourniquet.
Take a bleeding-control or Red Cross first-aid course.

sourceReferences.title: Life-Threatening External Bleeding
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Strong public-facing severe bleeding protocol.

sourceReferences.title: Severe Bleeding Online Course
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Evidence-based bleeding control training principles.

guides.title: Caring for Minor Cuts and Scrapes
guides.existingGuideReference: Minor Cuts and Scrapes: Clean and Cover
guides.quickAnswer: Rinse the wound with clean water, remove visible dirt, cover it with a clean dressing, and watch for infection.
guides.whenToUse: Use for shallow cuts and scrapes with slow, controllable bleeding and no deep tissue or large gap visible.
preferredAction: Gently clean with clean running water.
Cover with a sterile or clean non-stick dressing.
Monitor the wound daily for infection signs.
backupAction: If clean running water is not available, use the cleanest bottled or saline water you have and gently remove obvious dirt.
steps: Wash or sanitize your hands if possible.
Apply gentle pressure if minor bleeding continues.
Rinse under clean water.
Clean around the wound with mild soap.
Pat the surrounding skin dry.
Cover with a clean dressing.
Change the dressing daily or when wet or dirty.
warnings: Dirty wounds and high-risk contamination increase infection risk.
People with diabetes or poor circulation may need earlier medical review.
whatNotToDo: Do not repeatedly use full-strength hydrogen peroxide, alcohol, or iodine inside the wound.
Do not close deep or heavily contaminated wounds in the field.
redFlags: Gaping wounds, bites, embedded objects, visible bone/tendon/fat, uncontrollable bleeding, or high-risk contamination need urgent medical advice.
preparednessTips: Keep dressings, saline or clean water, and mild soap in your kit.
Keep tetanus vaccination up to date.

sourceReferences.title: Cuts and Puncture Wounds
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Public-friendly, conservative wound-cleaning guidance.

guides.title: Wound Infection: Red Flags
guides.quickAnswer: Watch for spreading redness, swelling, warmth, worsening pain, pus, red streaks, or fever, and seek care early if they appear.
guides.whenToUse: Use when monitoring any wound over hours to days, especially dirty wounds, bites, punctures, or wounds in people with chronic illness.
preferredAction: Keep the wound clean and dry and inspect it daily.
Seek medical care promptly if infection signs develop or spread.
backupAction: If care is delayed, continue gentle cleaning and dry dressings while seeking the earliest possible medical review.
steps: Inspect the wound daily in good light.
Look for spreading redness or swelling.
Check for warmth, worsening pain, or throbbing.
Look for pus or foul-smelling drainage.
Monitor for fever or feeling unwell.
Seek medical care if symptoms worsen or spread.
warnings: Infections near joints, face, or in people with diabetes or immune problems can worsen quickly.
whatNotToDo: Do not tightly seal an infected or draining wound.
Do not start leftover antibiotics without professional advice.
redFlags: Rapidly spreading redness, red streaks, high fever, severe pain, or inability to move the area normally require urgent help.
preparednessTips: Keep a thermometer and spare dressings in your kit.
Use notes or photos to monitor changes over time.

sourceReferences.title: Wound Infection Warning Signs
sourceReferences.organization: Public health ministry / standard wound care guidance
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Common red-flag descriptions aligned with public first-aid practice.

guides.title: Dehydration: Signs and Immediate Care
guides.quickAnswer: Move to a cooler place and give small frequent sips of safe fluids if the person is awake and not vomiting.
guides.whenToUse: Use after heat exposure, vomiting, diarrhea, or prolonged exertion when someone is thirsty, weak, dizzy, or making very little dark urine.
preferredAction: Rest in a cool or shaded place and sip water or oral rehydration solution slowly.
Monitor for improvement versus worsening signs.
backupAction: If only limited fluids are available, use small frequent sips of the safest available drink and avoid alcohol.
steps: Check for dry mouth, dark urine, dizziness, fatigue, or reduced urination.
Move to shade or a cool area.
Loosen tight clothing.
Give small sips of water or oral rehydration solution if fully awake.
Monitor over the next 1–2 hours.
warnings: Children, older adults, and chronically ill people may worsen quickly.
Severe dehydration can cause shock and organ failure.
whatNotToDo: Do not force fluids into someone who is vomiting repeatedly, very drowsy, or confused.
Do not rely on thirst alone.
redFlags: No urine, confusion, fainting, repeated vomiting, seizures, or rapid worsening need urgent help.
preparednessTips: Carry oral rehydration salts or electrolyte packets.
Teach urine-color awareness as a simple hydration check.

sourceReferences.title: Signs of Dehydration
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Conservative public guidance on dehydration signs and when to seek help.

sourceReferences.title: Dehydration Symptoms
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Clear symptom guidance for public use.

guides.title: Heat Exhaustion: On-the-Spot Care
guides.quickAnswer: Move the person to a cooler place, loosen clothing, cool the body, and give fluids if they are fully awake and not vomiting.
guides.whenToUse: Use for heavy sweating, weakness, nausea, dizziness, headache, or cramps during or after heat exposure.
preferredAction: Move the person to shade or cooler air immediately.
Cool the body and give small sips of fluid if appropriate.
Watch for progression to heat stroke.
backupAction: If no building is available, use shade, wet cloths, and fanning.
steps: Move to shade or air conditioning.
Have the person lie down.
Loosen or remove excess clothing.
Apply cool wet cloths or spray with cool water and fan.
Offer cool water or oral rehydration solution in small sips if fully awake.
Seek urgent help if symptoms worsen or do not improve within about 30 minutes.
warnings: Heat exhaustion can progress to heat stroke.
Do not leave a confused, vomiting, or very weak person alone.
whatNotToDo: Do not give alcohol or very sugary drinks.
Do not let the person continue strenuous activity.
redFlags: Collapse, confusion, stopping sweating, seizures, chest pain, or breathing difficulty require urgent help.
preparednessTips: Plan shade, water, and rest breaks during hot weather.
Teach users to recognize early heat illness signs.

sourceReferences.title: First Aid for Heat Illness
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Public-facing heat illness first aid guidance.

sourceReferences.title: Heat Illness
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Concise symptom and first-aid guidance.

guides.title: Heat Stroke: Suspected Life-Threatening Emergency
guides.quickAnswer: Call emergency services, move the person to a cooler place, and cool the body aggressively. Do not give fluids to a confused or unconscious person.
guides.whenToUse: Use for confusion, seizures, collapse, or extremely hot skin after heat exposure.
preferredAction: Activate emergency services immediately.
Begin active cooling right away while help is on the way.
backupAction: If no immediate medical help is available, continue safe cooling and monitor breathing and responsiveness.
steps: Call emergency services.
Move the person to a cooler or shaded place.
Remove excess clothing.
Cool with wet cloths, cool water, and fanning.
Use cool packs at the neck, groin, and armpits if available.
If unconscious but breathing, place on their side and monitor airway and breathing.
warnings: Delay in cooling can be fatal.
Giving fluids to a confused or unconscious person risks choking or aspiration.
whatNotToDo: Do not just let the person rest in shade without active cooling.
Do not give fluids to someone who is confused, drowsy, or unconscious.
redFlags: Any suspected heat stroke requires urgent emergency help.
preparednessTips: Prevent heat illness by planning shade, water, rest breaks, and early recognition.

sourceReferences.title: Heat Stroke First Aid
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Clear emergency heat stroke guidance.

sourceReferences.title: First Aid for Heat Illness
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Public-health first aid guidance for heat emergencies.

guides.title: Hypothermia: Cold Exposure First Aid
guides.quickAnswer: Move the person to warmth, remove wet clothing, warm the body core gradually, and seek help for moderate or severe symptoms.
guides.whenToUse: Use for shivering, confusion, slurred speech, drowsiness, or other cold-exposure symptoms.
preferredAction: Move to warm, dry shelter and prevent further heat loss.
Warm the center of the body first while monitoring breathing.
backupAction: If no shelter is available, block wind, insulate from the ground, and share body heat if needed.
steps: Call emergency services for suspected moderate or severe hypothermia.
Move gently to a sheltered place.
Remove wet clothing.
Wrap in dry layers or blankets.
Warm the chest, neck, head, and groin first.
Offer warm non-alcoholic drinks only if fully awake and able to swallow.
Monitor breathing and responsiveness.
warnings: Rough handling can trigger dangerous heart rhythms in severe hypothermia.
Do not use aggressive hot rewarming methods without medical oversight.
whatNotToDo: Do not give alcohol or caffeine.
Do not vigorously rub or massage cold limbs in severe hypothermia.
redFlags: Confusion, stopped shivering, very slow breathing, rigidity, or unconsciousness require urgent help.
preparednessTips: Pack dry layers, emergency blankets, and waterproof outerwear.
Know local cold-weather shelter and emergency options.

sourceReferences.title: Hypothermia and Frostbite
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Public first-aid guidance for cold injuries.

sourceReferences.title: Hypothermia and Cold Injuries
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Conservative international first-aid principles.

guides.title: Frostbite: Local Cold Injury Care
guides.quickAnswer: Get to warmth, remove wet items, and rewarm the area gently only if it will not refreeze.
guides.whenToUse: Use for numb, pale, hard, or waxy skin on fingers, toes, nose, ears, or cheeks after cold exposure.
preferredAction: Protect the area from further cold and trauma.
Rewarm gradually with body heat or warm water only if refreezing is unlikely.
backupAction: If you cannot prevent refreezing, protect the area and delay active rewarming until you reach stable warmth.
steps: Move to a warm, dry area.
Remove wet clothing and jewelry.
Use body heat or warm water if safe from refreezing.
Cover loosely and protect the area after rewarming.
Seek medical care as soon as practical.
warnings: Refreezing after thawing can make damage much worse.
Rewarming can be painful; handle gently.
whatNotToDo: Do not rub with hands or snow.
Do not use direct dry heat like a stove or fire on numb skin.
redFlags: Large areas, blackened tissue, blisters, or frostbite plus hypothermia signs need urgent help.
preparednessTips: Use layered clothing, warm socks, gloves, and hats in cold environments.
Check companions for numbness or color changes early.

sourceReferences.title: Cold Injuries and Frostbite
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Conservative rewarming and protection guidance.

sourceReferences.title: Hypothermia and Frostbite
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Public-friendly first-aid steps.

guides.title: First Aid for Burns (Minor)
guides.quickAnswer: Cool the burn with cool running water for at least 20 minutes, remove tight items, and cover loosely with a clean non-stick dressing.
guides.whenToUse: Use for small, superficial or superficial-partial burns that do not involve critical areas extensively.
preferredAction: Cool with cool running water for at least 20 minutes.
Remove rings and tight items before swelling increases.
Cover loosely with a clean non-stick dressing.
backupAction: If no tap water is available, use clean cool water or repeated cool wet cloths.
steps: Stop the burning source.
Cool the area with cool water for 20 minutes.
Remove jewelry and tight items if not stuck.
Cover loosely with a clean dressing or plastic wrap.
Monitor for worsening or infection.
warnings: Even minor burns can worsen over time.
Children and older adults may be more vulnerable to complications.
whatNotToDo: Do not apply ice directly.
Do not use butter, oils, or thick ointments that trap heat.
Do not intentionally break blisters.
redFlags: Burns larger than the person’s palm, burns to face/hands/feet/genitals/major joints, electrical or chemical burns, or infection signs need urgent care.
preparednessTips: Keep clean water access, non-stick dressings, and plastic wrap in the kit.
Teach the 20-minute cooling rule.

sourceReferences.title: Burn First Aid
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Specialist burn first-aid guidance.

sourceReferences.title: Burns: How to Help
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Public-friendly burn-care guidance.

guides.title: Burns That Need Emergency Care
guides.quickAnswer: Large, deep, or critical-area burns, or burns from chemicals, electricity, or inhalation need urgent medical attention.
guides.whenToUse: Use for burns that are more than minor superficial injuries or if the burn affects breathing, critical areas, or large surface area.
preferredAction: Begin basic burn first aid while arranging urgent medical care or evacuation.
Prioritize airway and breathing if facial burns or smoke exposure are present.
backupAction: If evacuation is delayed, continue gentle cooling if appropriate and keep the area clean and loosely covered while monitoring airway and circulation.
steps: Assess size, depth, and location.
Call emergency services or arrange urgent transport.
Stop the burning source.
Cool with cool water for up to 20 minutes if still hot.
Remove tight items if not stuck.
Cover loosely and monitor breathing.
warnings: Serious burns can cause fluid loss, shock, and airway compromise.
Deep burns may hurt less but still be more dangerous.
whatNotToDo: Do not apply home remedies or thick creams to severe burns.
Do not delay transport after basic first aid is done.
redFlags: Deep burns, face/airway burns, breathing difficulty, large burns, electrical, lightning, or chemical burns require urgent care.
preparednessTips: Know where the nearest emergency care or burn-capable hospital is.
Teach quick burn classification by size and location.

sourceReferences.title: Burn First Aid and Burn Center Referral
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Specialist guidance for escalation criteria.

sourceReferences.title: Burns Guidance
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Public-facing burn first aid and when to seek help.

guides.title: Choking: Responsive Adult or Child
guides.quickAnswer: If the person cannot cough, speak, or breathe, give back blows and abdominal thrusts (or chest thrusts when appropriate) and call for help.
guides.whenToUse: Use when a responsive person shows signs of severe choking after eating or choking on an object.
preferredAction: If they cannot speak or breathe, start severe choking first aid right away and get emergency help called.
backupAction: If abdominal thrusts are inappropriate, use chest thrusts as recommended for pregnancy or body size considerations.
steps: Ask if the person is choking.
If severe choking is confirmed, call for help.
Lean them forward and give up to 5 firm back blows.
If still choking, give up to 5 abdominal thrusts or chest thrusts when appropriate.
Alternate until the object comes out or the person becomes unresponsive.
warnings: Use appropriate force and technique for size and age.
Move to unresponsive choking/CPR guidance if the person collapses.
whatNotToDo: Do not do blind finger sweeps in a responsive person.
Do not interrupt an effective cough with severe choking maneuvers.
redFlags: Any severe choking episode requires emergency help; persistent breathing difficulty after the object is removed also needs review.
preparednessTips: Encourage formal first-aid and CPR training.
Reduce choking risks for children with age-appropriate foods and objects.

sourceReferences.title: Choking First Aid
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Clear public choking response steps.

sourceReferences.title: Choking in Adults and Children
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Public guidance reflecting consensus resuscitation practice.

guides.title: Choking: Unresponsive Adult or Child
guides.quickAnswer: Call for help, start CPR with chest compressions, and check the mouth only for an object you can clearly see and remove easily.
guides.whenToUse: Use when a choking person becomes unresponsive or is found unresponsive after suspected choking.
preferredAction: Start CPR immediately and get emergency help and an AED if available.
Use compressions and airway checks rather than abdominal thrusts once the person is unresponsive.
backupAction: If you do not know full CPR, follow dispatcher instructions and continue compressions.
steps: Call for help and get an AED.
Place the person on a firm surface.
Check for breathing; if absent or gasping, begin CPR.
Give 30 chest compressions.
Before breaths, quickly look for a visible object and remove it only if easily accessible.
Continue cycles until help arrives or the person recovers.
warnings: Do not waste time on prolonged searching in the mouth.
Compressions take priority in an unresponsive person.
whatNotToDo: Do not do blind deep finger sweeps.
Do not stop CPR unless the person recovers or you cannot continue.
redFlags: Any unresponsive person with suspected airway blockage needs urgent emergency response.
preparednessTips: Teach CPR and AED use in first-aid training.
Know your local emergency number.

sourceReferences.title: Choking (Unconscious Adult or Child)
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Public-facing unresponsive choking and CPR distinction.

guides.title: Sprains and Suspected Fractures
guides.quickAnswer: If you are unsure whether it is a sprain or fracture, treat it as a fracture: immobilize, avoid weight-bearing, use cold packs, and get medical assessment.
guides.whenToUse: Use after a fall, twist, or impact with swelling, deformity, severe pain, or difficulty moving or bearing weight.
preferredAction: Immobilize the limb in the position found and arrange assessment.
Use cold packs and avoid further use of the limb.
backupAction: If splints are unavailable, use rolled blankets or clothing to keep the limb still during transport.
steps: Check for deformity, severe pain, numbness, or inability to use the limb.
If fracture is suspected, immobilize above and below the injury in the position found.
Use a wrapped cold pack for up to 20 minutes.
Elevate if comfortable and safe.
Arrange medical evaluation.
warnings: Open fractures and circulation problems are emergencies.
Strong pain or deformity can hide nerve or blood-flow injury.
whatNotToDo: Do not try to straighten a deformed limb.
Do not use tight compression over a suspected fracture or open wound.
redFlags: Visible bone, gross deformity, pale/cold/numb limb, suspected spine injury, or inability to bear weight may need urgent help.
preparednessTips: Pack triangular bandages, elastic wraps, and simple splint materials.
Teach basic immobilization.

sourceReferences.title: Orthopedic First Aid Principles
sourceReferences.organization: Red Cross / standard first-aid aligned guidance
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Conservative immobilization-focused approach, but source specificity should be improved.

guides.title: Allergic Reaction and Anaphylaxis Warning Signs
guides.quickAnswer: Watch for hives plus swelling of the face or throat, trouble breathing, or feeling faint. Treat suspected anaphylaxis as an emergency.
guides.whenToUse: Use after exposure to foods, stings, medications, or unknown triggers, especially in someone with known allergies.
preferredAction: Recognize anaphylaxis early, call emergency services, and help with prescribed epinephrine if available and you are trained or permitted.
backupAction: If no epinephrine is available, keep the person at rest, watch breathing, and seek urgent emergency care.
steps: Ask about known allergies.
Look for hives, facial swelling, or generalized itching.
Check for wheeze, throat tightness, swallowing difficulty, or voice changes.
Monitor dizziness, faintness, or weak rapid pulse.
Call emergency services for breathing or circulation involvement.
Help use an auto-injector if available and appropriate.
Keep the person in the most tolerable safe position and monitor closely.
warnings: Anaphylaxis can progress very quickly.
Antihistamines alone do not treat airway or circulation collapse.
whatNotToDo: Do not wait for all symptoms to appear before acting.
Do not let the person stand or walk if faint.
redFlags: Breathing difficulty, throat tightness, tongue swelling, fainting, or rapid progression of symptoms require urgent help.
preparednessTips: People at risk should carry two auto-injectors if prescribed.
Store allergy information and emergency contacts.

sourceReferences.title: Anaphylaxis and Allergic Reaction Recognition
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Good public-health recognition principles, but the batch would benefit from stronger named primary sources.
